_comment: S11 cost worksheet (storage contract §7). The number the company watches -- cost per billion
events retained -- as a MEASURED output, not a slide. Bytes per row are measured by the
two-tier bench accounting (crates/prism-engine/src/bench.rs; committed baselines.json), then
scaled to per-million and per-billion events. A byte is a byte; the dollar cost is a function
of the backend's storage price, which is why this worksheet reports BYTES and leaves the price
multiplication to the operator with their contract.
Charter honesty: this is BACKEND-CONDITIONAL. These bytes are the on-disk/on-object footprint,
which is the same on a local object store and on S3; the REQUEST and EGRESS costs, and any
compression the backend applies, are not, and are tagged as measured against the local backend
until MinIO/S3 is exercised (the filed increment).

bytes_per_row._note: pq_m bytes for the PQ codes; dim*4 for the exact float32 vectors; the rest is the scalar columns and the bodies. rerank_tier_multiple = exact / pq = 32x.

tiers._rule: Hot tier = the scan path (PQ codes + scalar columns), always resident/cached and read on every query. Cold tier = the rerank path (exact vectors) plus the bodies, fetched only for survivors/returns. Bodies currently live inside scalars_and_text in the bench accounting; separating them out is a filed refinement, so the split below assigns exact_vectors to cold and the rest to hot, which is the conservative reading for cold-tier egress.

per_billion_events._note: 1e9 events x 548.36 B/event = 548.36 GB total retained. The cold tier -- the expensive-to-egress exact vectors -- is 256 GB of that. The scan tier a query always touches is the 292 GB hot half.

request_counts._rule: Cold-tier object requests per query, from Counters.object_requests: one per part whose exact vectors are fetched, coalesced ranged reads within a part counting as one. A query touching P eligible parts issues <= P cold requests; the fetch budget (§6) bounds the bytes those requests move.
